mechanism: Concentrated supplier base in few regions means localized disruptions (natural disasters, geopolitical events, pandemics) simultaneously affect a large share of supply capacity.
High-betweenness nodes serve as chokepoints in supply networks. Their failure disconnects large portions of the network, triggering cascading downstream failures.
Multiple suppliers for critical inputs provide redundancy. If one fails, others absorb demand. Effectiveness depends on suppliers being geographically and operationally independent.

High spatial autocorrelation in trade flows indicates geographic clustering of trade activity. Disruptions in high-cluster regions propagate to neighboring regions through shared infrastructure and common suppliers.
Visibility into multi-tier supplier operations enables earlier disruption detection and faster activation of contingency plans, reducing time to recover.
Geopolitical instability (sanctions, trade wars, conflicts) restricts trade through tariffs, embargoes, export controls, and increased transaction costs.
Demand amplification upstream causes order volatility that suppliers respond to with variable production schedules, creating lead time unpredictability. Feedback loops amplify the effect.
Higher safety stock directly extends the duration a node can operate without upstream supply. TTS is a function of buffer inventory and demand rate.
Financially healthy suppliers are less likely to fail, can invest in resilience measures, and maintain capacity buffers. Financial distress precedes operational failure.
Port congestion creates variable vessel waiting times and container dwell times, introducing unpredictable delays into supply chain lead times.
Aggregate disruptions increase global supply chain pressure measured by transportation costs, delivery times, and order backlogs that compose the GSCPI.
REI is a key component of overall supply chain vulnerability assessment, measuring the gap between recovery time and survival time at each node.
Geographic proximity means disruptions spread spatially (through shared infrastructure, weather events, policy changes) in addition to propagating through network linkages.
80% API production concentrated in China/India. Export bans, factory shutdowns, or logistics disruptions in either country immediately affect global pharmaceutical supply.
Uncertainty about future tariffs, sanctions, and export controls causes firms to delay or reduce cross-border trade, particularly for intermediate goods that constitute supply chain inputs.
Clean energy technology deployment multiplies demand for lithium, cobalt, rare earths, and other minerals faster than supply can diversify, deepening dependency on concentrated sources.
High substitutability means alternative suppliers can be activated quickly. Low substitutability (long qualification times, unique specs) extends recovery time dramatically.
Food supply disruptions (crop failures, export bans) increase geopolitical instability through food price spikes, social unrest, and retaliatory trade measures (e.g., 2010 Russian wheat export ban).
Climate change increases frequency and severity of extreme weather events (floods, hurricanes, droughts) that damage supply chain infrastructure, disrupt logistics, and reduce agricultural output.
Each additional supply chain tier adds an information barrier. Firms rarely have visibility beyond Tier 1-2 suppliers, making deep supply chains opaque to risk assessment.

Digital supply chain infrastructure creates new attack vectors: ransomware disrupts operations, data breaches compromise logistics data, IoT vulnerabilities enable physical disruption through cyber means.
Critical minerals are geologically concentrated in few countries. High dependency on these minerals forces supply chain reliance on concentrated geographic sources.
Relocating production closer to end markets diversifies geographic exposure and reduces reliance on distant, concentrated supply regions.
Disruption propagation through supply chain tiers (ripple effect) is the mechanism by which localized failures expand into large-scale cascading failures.
High-betweenness suppliers may earn higher margins from their critical position, but also face higher operational demands and risk exposure.
Extreme fab concentration (92% in TSMC for advanced nodes) means any disruption to a single facility cascades through global electronics, automotive, and defense supply chains.
Blockage or congestion at maritime chokepoints forces rerouting that adds 10-15 days transit time, introducing unpredictable lead time variance for dependent supply chains.
Pandemics create simultaneous supply disruption and demand shock (panic buying, demand shifts), amplifying order volatility far beyond normal bullwhip levels.
Regulatory pressure (EU CBAM, SEC climate disclosure) and carbon pricing create financial risk from supply chain emissions, adding a regulatory vulnerability dimension.
Strong defense SCRM practices (trusted foundry, component authentication, C-SCRM) reduce cyber attack surface in supply chain, but imperfect detection means correlation is moderate.
